subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Listen carefully: BECAUSE. Say it back to me. Now, can you spell 'because'? Say each letter!
correct_response: B-E-C-A-U-S-E! Brilliant! Remember: Big Elephants Can Always Understand Small Elephants! That spells BECAUSE!
incorrect_response: Tricky one! BECAUSE is spelled B-E-C-A-U-S-E. There's a great way to remember it: Big Elephants Can Always Understand Small Elephants! The first letter of each word spells BECAUSE!
hint: There's a famous trick for this one: Big Elephants Can Always Understand Small Elephants. Take the first letter of each word!
mnemonic: Big Elephants Can Always Understand Small Elephants
metacognitive_prompt: Did the mnemonic help you remember? Making up silly sentences is a great spelling trick!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Listen: BEAUTIFUL. Say it. Beautiful! Now spell it out for me, letter by letter!
correct_response: B-E-A-U-T-I-F-U-L! You spelled that beautifully! Just like you!
incorrect_response: That's a tough one! BEAUTIFUL is B-E-A-U-T-I-F-U-L. Remember it starts with 'beau' which is French for handsome! And there's a helpful phrase: Big Elephants Are Ugly To Insects Flying Under Logs!
hint: It starts with B-E-A-U. Think of it in chunks: beau-ti-ful. The first bit is 'beau' like a French word!
mnemonic: Big Elephants Are Ugly To Insects Flying Under Logs

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Here's your word: FRIEND. My best friend! Can you spell 'friend' for me?
correct_response: F-R-I-E-N-D! Perfect! Remember: a friend is a FRIEND to the END! You can see 'end' hiding inside 'friend'!
incorrect_response: Nearly! FRIEND is spelled F-R-I-E-N-D. The tricky part is the 'ie' in the middle. Here's how to remember: FRI-END. A friend is a friend to the END! See 'end' at the end?
hint: Look for a small word hiding inside: fri-END. A friend stays with you to the... end!
mnemonic: A friend is a friend to the END
metacognitive_prompt: Can you spot the word 'end' hiding inside 'friend'? Finding words within words is a great strategy!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Your word is: SAID. She said hello! Can you spell 'said'?
correct_response: S-A-I-D! Well done! 'Said' is a tricky one because the 'ai' doesn't make its usual sound!
incorrect_response: SAID is spelled S-A-I-D. It's tricky because 'ai' usually says 'ay' like in rain, but in 'said' it sounds like 'eh'. It's a rebel word! S-A-I-D.
hint: It has four letters. It starts with S and ends with D, and 'ai' is hiding in the middle even though it sounds like 'eh'!
mnemonic: Silly Ants In Dresses

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Spell the word: THEIR. Their house is big. Can you spell 'their'?
correct_response: T-H-E-I-R! Spot on! 'Their' means belonging to them. And look - 'the' is hiding at the start! The-ir!
incorrect_response: THEIR is T-H-E-I-R. It means 'belonging to them'. A good trick: 'the' is hiding at the start! THE-IR. Their ball, their house, their turn!
hint: A very common word hides at the start of this word. THE... then add two more letters: I and R!
mnemonic: THEIR has THE hiding inside - THE-IR belongs to them

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Listen: DOES. She does her homework. Can you spell 'does'?
correct_response: D-O-E-S! Great job! 'Does' is sneaky because it sounds like 'duz' but it's spelled with an O and an E!
incorrect_response: DOES is spelled D-O-E-S. Even though it sounds like 'duz', it's actually D-O-E-S. Think of it as 'do' with 'es' on the end. Do + es = does!
hint: Start with the word 'do' and add 'es' to the end. Do + es!
mnemonic: DO + ES = DOES

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Your word is: ONCE. Once upon a time! Spell 'once' for me!
correct_response: O-N-C-E! Wonderful! Once upon a time, there was a brilliant speller called Reuben!
incorrect_response: ONCE is O-N-C-E. It starts with O even though it sounds like it starts with 'w'! That's what makes it tricky. Once = O-N-C-E. Once upon a time!
hint: It sounds like it starts with W, but it actually starts with the letter O! O-N then C-E!
mnemonic: Only Naughty Children Eat - ONCE
metacognitive_prompt: Were you surprised that 'once' starts with O and not W? English can be tricky like that!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Spell this one: WHERE. Where are you going? Can you spell 'where'?
correct_response: W-H-E-R-E! Brilliant! And look - 'here' is hiding inside 'where'! W-HERE!
incorrect_response: WHERE is spelled W-H-E-R-E. Here's a great trick: 'here' is hiding inside 'where'! W + HERE = WHERE. Where? Over here!
hint: Think about the word 'here'. Now put a W and an H in front of it... w-here!
mnemonic: WHERE has HERE hiding inside it

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Here's your word: AGAIN. Let's do it again! Can you spell 'again'?
correct_response: A-G-A-I-N! Well done! 'A-gain'. And you can practise this one again and again and again!
incorrect_response: AGAIN is A-G-A-I-N. Break it into two bits: a-gain. A then GAIN. A-G-A-I-N. Let's try it again!
hint: Split it into two parts: a + gain. A... then GAIN like gaining points in a game!
mnemonic: A + GAIN = AGAIN

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Last one in this set: MANY. There are many stars! Can you spell 'many'?
correct_response: M-A-N-Y! Star explorer! You know so many tricky words now!
incorrect_response: MANY is M-A-N-Y. Only four letters! The tricky part is that it sounds like 'menny' but it's spelled with an A, not an E! M-A-N-Y.
hint: Only four letters! It starts with M-A-N and ends with Y. The tricky bit is the A because it sounds like 'e'!
mnemonic: MAN + Y = MANY (many men, man-y)
metacognitive_prompt: What pattern do you notice? Sometimes letters don't make the sound you'd expect!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Listen: COULD. I could do it! Spell 'could' for me, letter by letter!
correct_response: C-O-U-L-D! Awesome! Notice the sneaky silent L in there? Could, would, should - they all have that hidden L!
incorrect_response: COULD is C-O-U-L-D. There's a silent L hiding in there! You can't hear it but it's there. Could, would, should - they're a family and they ALL have a silent L!
hint: It sounds like 'cood' but there's a silent letter hiding in the middle. C-O-U then a letter you can't hear, then D!
mnemonic: O U Lucky Duck - could, would, should all have OULD

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Now spell: SHOULD. You should try! Can you spell 'should'?
correct_response: S-H-O-U-L-D! Perfect! Just like 'could' but starting with SH! The 'ould' family strikes again!
incorrect_response: SHOULD is S-H-O-U-L-D. Remember the 'ould' family? Could, would, should - they all end in O-U-L-D with that sneaky silent L! S-H then OULD!
hint: It starts with SH (the quiet sound!) and then has the same ending as 'could'. SH + OULD!
mnemonic: SH + OULD (same family as could and would)
metacognitive_prompt: Do you notice a pattern with could, would, and should? They're like a spelling family!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Spell: WOULD. I would like ice cream! Spell 'would' for me!
correct_response: W-O-U-L-D! Hat trick! You've got could, should AND would! The whole OULD family!
incorrect_response: WOULD is W-O-U-L-D. It's the third member of the OULD family! Could, should, would - they all have O-U-L-D at the end. W + OULD!
hint: Same ending as 'could' and 'should'! Start with W and then add OULD. The whole family!
mnemonic: W + OULD (the OULD triplets: could, should, would)

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Here's a big one: PEOPLE. Lots of people! Spell 'people' for me!
correct_response: P-E-O-P-L-E! Amazing! That's a tough word and you nailed it!
incorrect_response: PEOPLE is P-E-O-P-L-E. The tricky part is that 'eo' in the middle - it doesn't sound like you'd expect! Think: People Eat Oranges, People Like Eggs!
hint: Break it into chunks: PEO-PLE. It starts with P-E-O and ends with P-L-E. The first bit sounds weird but that's what makes it tricky!
mnemonic: People Eat Oranges, People Like Eggs

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Your word is: WATER. A glass of water! Spell 'water'!
correct_response: W-A-T-E-R! Splash! You got it! Five letters, nice and clear!
incorrect_response: WATER is W-A-T-E-R. This one's tricky because the A doesn't quite sound like a normal A sound. W-A-T-E-R. Think of water as 'wat-er' - two chunks!
hint: Break it into two chunks: WAT-ER. Five letters in total. W-A-T then E-R!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Spell this: MONEY. Pocket money! Can you spell 'money'?
correct_response: M-O-N-E-Y! Ka-ching! You're a spelling millionaire! Five letters!
incorrect_response: MONEY is M-O-N-E-Y. The tricky part is the O - it sounds like 'uh' but it's an O! Think MON-EY, like Monday without the 'day'!
hint: Five letters. Starts like 'Monday' - MON - then add E-Y at the end!
mnemonic: MON from Monday + EY

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Try this one: EVERY. Every day! Spell 'every' for me!
correct_response: E-V-E-R-Y! Excellent! There's a word hiding inside: EVER-Y. Ever + Y!
incorrect_response: EVERY is E-V-E-R-Y. Think of it as EVER + Y! The word 'ever' is hiding inside: E-V-E-R, then just add a Y! Every!
hint: The word 'ever' is hiding inside this word! EVER + one more letter at the end...
mnemonic: EVER + Y = EVERY
metacognitive_prompt: What word can you find hiding inside 'every'?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Spell: CHILDREN. The children played outside! Can you spell 'children'?
correct_response: C-H-I-L-D-R-E-N! Fantastic! Eight letters! And look - 'child' is hiding at the start!
incorrect_response: CHILDREN is C-H-I-L-D-R-E-N. Here's a good trick: 'child' is hiding at the start! CHILD + REN. Child-ren!
hint: Start with the word 'child' - C-H-I-L-D - then add three more letters: R-E-N!
mnemonic: CHILD + REN = CHILDREN

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Your word is: DIFFERENT. That's a different colour! Spell 'different'!
correct_response: D-I-F-F-E-R-E-N-T! Nine letters! Did you remember the double F? Brilliant spelling!
incorrect_response: DIFFERENT is D-I-F-F-E-R-E-N-T. Watch out for the double F in the middle! DIF-FER-ENT. Three chunks: dif, fer, ent!
hint: Three chunks: DIF-FER-ENT. Be careful - there are two F's in the first chunk! DIF-F...

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Last one: THOUGHT. I thought about it! Can you spell 'thought'?
correct_response: T-H-O-U-G-H-T! Brilliant! That's a really tricky one with the 'ough' pattern. You're a champion!
incorrect_response: THOUGHT is T-H-O-U-G-H-T. The tricky part is 'ough' - it looks like lots of letters but it's just one sound! TH + OUGHT. Think: I bought what I thought!
hint: Start with TH, then the tricky 'ough' bit (O-U-G-H), then T at the end. TH-OUGH-T!
mnemonic: TH + OUGHT (I ought to have thought!)
metacognitive_prompt: The 'ough' pattern appears in lots of words: thought, bought, brought. Can you think of others?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What's the magic E doing in the word 'cake'? C-A-K-E. The E is at the end but it changes something. What does it do?
correct_answers: makes the a say its name
correct_response: That's right! The magic E at the end reaches over the K and makes the A say its name! 'Ay!' Without the E it would just be 'cak'!
incorrect_response: The magic E is a sneaky letter! It sits quietly at the end and makes the A say its name - 'ay' instead of 'a'. Without the magic E, cake would be 'cak'. The E changes everything!
hint: Think about the A. Without the E, it would say its short sound 'a'. But with the E there, the A says...
metacognitive_prompt: What pattern do you notice? The E is silent but it changes the vowel sound!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: If I take the word 'kit' and add a magic E to the end, what word do I get? K-I-T... add an E... K-I-T-E!
correct_response: Kite! K-I-T becomes K-I-T-E! The magic E changed the 'ih' sound to 'eye'! Kit becomes kite! Go fly a kite!
incorrect_response: KIT with a magic E becomes KITE! The E makes the I say its name: 'eye' instead of 'ih'. So 'kit' turns into 'kite' - like a kite flying in the sky!
hint: The E is going to change how the I sounds. Instead of 'ih' it will say 'eye'. K-eye-t...

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Listen to these two words: 'hop' and 'hope'. What's different about how they're spelled and how they sound?
correct_answers: hope has e on the end
the e makes the o say its name
correct_response: Exactly! 'Hope' has a magic E that makes the O say its name - 'oh' instead of 'o'. Hop... hope! One little E changes everything!
incorrect_response: Great question to think about! HOP has a short O sound: 'o'. But HOPE has a magic E at the end that makes the O say its name: 'oh'. Hop to hope! The E is the magic ingredient!
hint: Look at the end of each word. One has an extra letter. What letter is it, and what does it do to the O sound?
metacognitive_prompt: What pattern do you notice about magic E? It's always silent but always powerful!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Can you think of a word that uses the 'u-e' split digraph? Like 'huge' - the magic E makes the U say its name! Can you give me another u-e word?
correct_response: Brilliant! That's a perfect u-e word! The magic E makes the U say 'yoo'! You really understand split digraphs!
incorrect_response: The u-e split digraph is in words like cube, cute, tube, and tune. The magic E at the end makes the U say its name: 'yoo'. A cube is a c-yoo-b! Can you hear it?
hint: Think of a 3D shape like a square box... it's a c-yoo-b... a cube! C-U-B-E!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: I'm going to say a word and you tell me if it has a magic E split digraph or not. Ready? The word is: SMILE. Does it have a split digraph?
correct_response: Yes it does! S-M-I-L-E! The E at the end reaches over the L and makes the I say 'eye'! Sm-eye-l! Smile!
incorrect_response: Yes, SMILE has the i-e split digraph! S-M-I-L-E. Even though L is between the I and the E, the magic E still reaches over and makes the I say its name. Sm-EYE-l!
hint: Listen to the I sound in smile. Does it say 'ih' or 'eye'? If it says 'eye', there might be a magic E...

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Fill in the missing word! I have a word that rhymes with 'lake' and you eat it at birthday parties. It's a... what? Spell it!
correct_response: CAKE! C-A-K-E! Happy birthday! The a-e split digraph makes the A say 'ay'. Cake, lake, bake, make - they're all in the same family!
incorrect_response: It's CAKE! C-A-K-E. The a-e split digraph is working hard here! The magic E makes the A say 'ay'. And look: cake, lake, bake, make - they all have the a-e pattern!
hint: It rhymes with lake and bake, and it's sweet and delicious with candles on top at a birthday party!
metacognitive_prompt: Can you think of other words in the '-ake' family? They all use the a-e split digraph!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What happens if I take the magic E away from 'time'? T-I-M-E becomes T-I-M. What would that sound like?
correct_response: Tim! Like the name Tim! Without the E, the I goes back to its short sound 'ih'. Time becomes Tim! The magic E makes all the difference!
incorrect_response: Without the magic E, TIME becomes TIM! The I changes from 'eye' back to 'ih'. T-EYE-M becomes T-IH-M. Tim! See how powerful that little E is?
hint: Without the E, the I will say its short sound 'ih' instead of 'eye'. T-ih-m... sounds like a name!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Quick fire! Which split digraph is in this word - a-e, i-e, o-e, or u-e? The word is: BONE!
correct_response: O-E! B-O-N-E! The magic E makes the O say 'oh'! Bone! Well spotted!
incorrect_response: BONE has the o-e split digraph! B-O-N-E. The magic E reaches over the N and makes the O say its name: 'oh'. B-OH-N! Bone!
hint: Listen to the vowel sound in bone: 'oh'. Which vowel says 'oh' as its name? That's your split digraph!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Here's a spelling pattern! Words like 'bridge' end in D-G-E. Bridge! The 'dge' comes after a SHORT vowel sound. Can you spell 'bridge'?
correct_response: B-R-I-D-G-E! Spot on! The short 'i' sound means we need DGE at the end. Bridge! You've cracked the code!
incorrect_response: BRIDGE is B-R-I-D-G-E. The rule is: after a short vowel sound (like the short 'i' in bridge), we use DGE. After a long vowel, we just use GE. Bridge has a short 'ih' so it's DGE!
hint: Listen to the vowel: 'ih' in bridge is a SHORT sound. Short vowel + 'j' sound = DGE! B-R-I then D-G-E!
mnemonic: Short vowel = DGE (the D is a bodyguard protecting the short vowel!)

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Spell the word 'badge'. Like a scout badge or a police badge! Remember the DGE rule!
correct_response: B-A-D-G-E! You've earned a spelling badge! Short 'a' sound, so we use DGE!
incorrect_response: BADGE is B-A-D-G-E. The 'a' in badge is a SHORT vowel sound, so we use DGE at the end. Remember: short vowel = DGE!
hint: The 'a' sound in badge is short (like 'a' in cat). Short vowel means we need D-G-E at the end!
metacognitive_prompt: What's the rule? Short vowel before the 'j' sound means...

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Now spell 'fudge'. Yummy chocolate fudge! Short vowel or long vowel? DGE or GE?
correct_response: F-U-D-G-E! Delicious spelling! The 'u' is short, so it's DGE. You really know this pattern!
incorrect_response: FUDGE is F-U-D-G-E. The 'u' in fudge is a short vowel sound, so we use DGE! If the vowel were long (like 'huge'), we'd just use GE. Short = DGE!
hint: Say the vowel sound: 'uh' in fudge. Is that a short or long U? Short means DGE!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Now here's the other pattern! Spell 'huge'. That dinosaur was HUGE! Is it DGE or just GE this time?
correct_response: H-U-G-E! Just GE this time, not DGE! Because the U in huge is a LONG vowel sound - 'yoo'. Long vowel = just GE!
incorrect_response: HUGE is H-U-G-E - just GE, not DGE! Why? Because the U says its name 'yoo' - it's a LONG vowel. Long vowel = just GE. Short vowel = DGE. That's the rule!
hint: Listen to the U: 'yoo' in huge. That's a LONG vowel sound. Long vowel means we just need G-E, no D!
metacognitive_prompt: Can you explain the DGE vs GE rule in your own words?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Spell 'age'. What age are you? Is this one DGE or GE?
correct_response: A-G-E! Just three letters! The A says its name 'ay' - that's a long vowel, so just GE!
incorrect_response: AGE is just A-G-E! Three little letters. The A says 'ay' - its name - which is a LONG vowel sound. Long vowel = just GE, no D needed!
hint: How does the A sound? 'Ay' - like the letter name. That's a long vowel, so you only need...

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Challenge round! Is it 'ledge' or 'lege'? I stood on the ledge! Which spelling is correct and why?
correct_response: LEDGE with DGE! L-E-D-G-E! The E in ledge makes a short 'eh' sound, so we need the D bodyguard! DGE! You really understand this rule!
incorrect_response: It's LEDGE - L-E-D-G-E! The E vowel in ledge is SHORT ('eh'), so we need DGE. Remember: short vowel = DGE, long vowel = just GE. The D is like a bodyguard for the short vowel!
hint: Say the vowel sound: 'eh' in ledge. Short or long? Remember the rule: short vowel needs the D bodyguard!
metacognitive_prompt: How do you decide between DGE and GE? What do you check first?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Knock knock! Who's there? The word KNOCK has a SILENT letter at the start! K-N-O-C-K. Can you spell 'knock'?
correct_response: K-N-O-C-K! The K is silent! You can't hear it but it's there! Knock knock... who's there? A brilliant speller!
incorrect_response: KNOCK is K-N-O-C-K. The K at the start is completely silent! We say 'nock' but spell it 'knock'. When K comes before N, the K goes quiet! K-N-O-C-K!
hint: It starts with K-N but the K is shy and silent! You only hear the N. Then O-C-K.
mnemonic: The K in KN words is a Kool Ninja - silent and sneaky!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Spell the word 'knife'. Be careful with your knife and fork! There's a silent letter at the start!
correct_response: K-N-I-F-E! Another silent K! The KN pattern strikes again! Knife, knock, knee, know - all have that sneaky silent K!
incorrect_response: KNIFE is K-N-I-F-E. Silent K again! Whenever you see KN at the start, the K is silent. We say 'nife' but spell it 'knife'. And there's a magic E at the end too!
hint: Same silent letter as 'knock'! K is silent before N. Then I-F-E with a magic E!
metacognitive_prompt: What pattern do you notice with knock, knife, knee, know? They all start with...

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Write starts with a SILENT W! W-R-I-T-E! The W is there but you can't hear it! Can you spell 'write'?
correct_response: W-R-I-T-E! That silent W is a tricky one! When W comes before R, the W goes silent. Write, wrong, wrap - all have silent W!
incorrect_response: WRITE is W-R-I-T-E. The W at the start is silent! When W comes before R, you only hear the R. We say 'rite' but spell it 'write'. Plus there's a magic E making the I long!
hint: Just like K is silent before N, W is silent before R! So start with W-R even though you only hear 'r'!
mnemonic: The W in WR words is Wonderfully silent - it's Wrapped up quiet!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Spell 'wrong'. That answer is wrong! Remember the silent letter rule for WR!
correct_response: W-R-O-N-G! You got it RIGHT! The W is silent. Write, wrong, wrap, wrist - the WR family!
incorrect_response: WRONG is W-R-O-N-G. Don't forget the silent W at the start! WR words are like the KN words - there's always a silent letter hiding at the beginning!
hint: Starts with the silent W before R! W-R then O-N-G!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Here's the third silent letter pattern: GN! Like in 'gnaw'. A dog gnaws a bone! The G is silent. Spell 'gnaw'!
correct_response: G-N-A-W! The G is silent, just like the K in knock and the W in write! Three families of silent letters!
incorrect_response: GNAW is G-N-A-W. The G at the start is silent! When G comes before N, the G stays quiet. We say 'naw' but spell it 'gnaw'. A dog gnaws a bone!
hint: It's the GN pattern - the G is silent before N! G-N-A-W but you only hear 'naw'.

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Spell 'knee'. You bumped your knee! Which silent letter family is this - KN, WR, or GN?
correct_response: K-N-E-E! The KN family! Silent K before N! And look - two E's at the end for the long 'ee' sound! Star explorer!
incorrect_response: KNEE is K-N-E-E. It's the KN family - silent K! And there are two E's at the end making the long 'ee' sound. K(silent)-N-E-E! Knee!
hint: It's the same silent letter pattern as knock and knife! K-N at the start, the K is silent. Then add two E's!
metacognitive_prompt: You've learned three silent letter patterns now: KN, WR, and GN. Can you remember a word for each?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Time for suffix magic! Add '-ing' to the word 'run'. R-U-N plus I-N-G. But wait - something special happens! What do you get?
correct_response: RUNNING with double N! R-U-N-N-I-N-G! When a short word ends in one consonant after a short vowel, you DOUBLE the consonant before adding -ing! Run becomes running!
incorrect_response: It's RUNNING - R-U-N-N-I-N-G! We have to double the N! The rule is: short vowel + single consonant = double that consonant before adding -ing. The short 'u' in run means we double the N!
hint: Listen to the vowel in 'run' - it's a short 'u'. When there's a short vowel before one consonant, you need to DOUBLE that consonant! R-U-N-N...
mnemonic: Short vowel, single consonant? DOUBLE UP before adding the suffix!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Add '-ed' to 'hike'. H-I-K-E plus E-D. What happens to the E that's already there?
correct_response: HIKED! H-I-K-E-D! You drop the E and add -ED! Because the word already ends in E, you don't need two! Hike becomes hiked!
incorrect_response: It's HIKED - H-I-K-E-D! When a word already ends in E (like hike), you just add D, not ED. You drop the extra E! Hike + d = hiked! Not 'hikeed'!
hint: Hike already ends in E. If you added -ed you'd have two E's in a row! So just drop the E and add -ed. Or think of it as just adding D!
mnemonic: Drop the E before adding -ED or -ING!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Add '-ing' to 'swim'. S-W-I-M plus I-N-G. What happens? Think about the doubling rule!
correct_response: SWIMMING with double M! S-W-I-M-M-I-N-G! Short 'i' vowel + one consonant M = double it! Swimming! Splash!
incorrect_response: It's SWIMMING - S-W-I-M-M-I-N-G! We double the M because of the short vowel rule. The 'i' in swim is short, so we double the M before adding -ing!
hint: Is the vowel in 'swim' short or long? It's short 'ih'! So what do you do to the M before adding -ing?
metacognitive_prompt: What's the rule for doubling? Can you explain it in your own words?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Add '-ing' to 'make'. M-A-K-E plus I-N-G. What happens to that magic E?
correct_response: MAKING! M-A-K-I-N-G! The E gets dropped when -ing arrives! Make becomes making! The -ing takes over!
incorrect_response: It's MAKING - M-A-K-I-N-G! When a word ends in magic E and you add -ing, the E gets dropped! Make - drop the E - add ing = making! Not 'makeing'!
hint: The magic E needs to leave to make room for -ing! Drop the E, then add -ING!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Add '-ed' to 'hop'. The rabbit hopped! H-O-P plus E-D. Think about the doubling rule!
correct_response: HOPPED with double P! H-O-P-P-E-D! Short 'o' + one consonant P = double it before adding -ed! The rabbit hopped!
incorrect_response: It's HOPPED - H-O-P-P-E-D! We double the P because the O in hop is a short vowel. Short vowel + single consonant = DOUBLE before adding -ed. Hop becomes hopped!
hint: Short 'o' in hop, then just one consonant P. What's the doubling rule? Double the P before adding -ed!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Add '-ed' to 'walk'. We walked to school! W-A-L-K plus E-D. Does anything special happen this time?
correct_response: WALKED! W-A-L-K-E-D! This time nothing special happens - just add -ed! No doubling needed because there are two consonants at the end!
incorrect_response: It's WALKED - W-A-L-K-E-D. No doubling this time! The doubling rule only applies when there's ONE consonant after a short vowel. Walk ends in two consonants (L-K), so just add -ed!
hint: Look at the end of walk: L-K. That's TWO consonants. The doubling rule only applies with ONE consonant. So just add -ed!
metacognitive_prompt: Why didn't we double anything this time? What's different about 'walk' compared to 'hop'?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Add '-ing' to 'sit'. S-I-T plus I-N-G. The cat is... what?
correct_response: SITTING! S-I-T-T-I-N-G! Double the T! Short 'i' + one consonant T = double it! The cat is sitting on the mat!
incorrect_response: It's SITTING - S-I-T-T-I-N-G! We double the T because the I in sit is short. Short vowel + single consonant = double before adding -ing! Sit becomes sitting!
hint: Short 'ih' vowel in sit, then just one T. Time to double! S-I-T-T then add -ING!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Add '-ing' to 'hope'. I am hoping for sunny weather! H-O-P-E plus I-N-G. What happens?
correct_response: HOPING! H-O-P-I-N-G! Drop the E, add -ing, and NO doubling! The O is long (magic E word), so no double P! Compare: hopping (short o, double p) vs hoping (long o, no double)!
incorrect_response: It's HOPING - H-O-P-I-N-G! Drop the magic E and add -ing. No doubling because the O is a LONG vowel (thanks to the magic E). It's HOPING not HOPPING! Hopping is from hop (short O)!
hint: Hope has a magic E making the O long. Drop the E, add -ing. The O is long so NO doubling! H-O-P-I-N-G!
mnemonic: Long vowel? No doubling! Short vowel? Double up!
metacognitive_prompt: This is a really important difference: hoping vs hopping! The magic E changes everything!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What does 'hopeful' mean? Break it down: hope... ful. If you're hopeful, you're full of... what?
correct_response: Full of hope! HOPE + FUL = HOPEFUL! The suffix '-ful' means 'full of'. Hopeful means full of hope! Cheerful means full of cheer!
incorrect_response: Hopeful means FULL OF HOPE! The suffix '-ful' means 'full of'. So cheerful = full of cheer, thankful = full of thanks, hopeful = full of hope! The suffix tells you the meaning!
hint: Look at the suffix: -FUL. It sounds like 'full'. So hopeful means full of...
mnemonic: -FUL means FULL OF something

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Add '-ness' to 'kind'. What word do you get? K-I-N-D plus N-E-S-S!
correct_response: KINDNESS! K-I-N-D-N-E-S-S! The suffix '-ness' turns the describing word 'kind' into a naming word - kindness! Kindness makes the world better!
incorrect_response: It's KINDNESS - K-I-N-D-N-E-S-S! Just add -NESS to the end of kind. The suffix '-ness' turns a describing word (kind) into a naming word (kindness). Simple!
hint: Just stick -NESS right onto the end of KIND. No tricks this time! Kind + ness = ...
mnemonic: -NESS turns a describing word into a naming word

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What does 'careless' mean? Break it apart: care... less. If someone is careless, they have less... what?
correct_response: Without care! CARE + LESS = CARELESS! The suffix '-less' means 'without'. Careless means without care! You're a wordsmith!
incorrect_response: Careless means WITHOUT CARE! The suffix '-less' means 'without' or 'having none of'. So careless = without care, homeless = without a home, fearless = without fear!
hint: The suffix '-less' means 'without'. So careless means without...
mnemonic: -LESS means WITHOUT something
metacognitive_prompt: Can you see how -FUL and -LESS are opposites? Hopeful vs hopeless!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Spell the word 'thankful'. I'm so thankful! T-H-A-N-K plus F-U-L. Careful - how many L's in '-ful'?
correct_response: T-H-A-N-K-F-U-L! And notice - just ONE L at the end! The word 'full' has two L's, but the suffix '-ful' only has ONE! Tricky!
incorrect_response: THANKFUL is T-H-A-N-K-F-U-L. Here's an important trick: the word 'full' has two L's, but when it's a suffix '-ful' it only has ONE L! Thankful, not thankfull!
hint: Be careful with the L! When '-ful' is a suffix (stuck on the end of a word), it only has ONE L, not two!
mnemonic: The suffix -FUL only has ONE L (it lost one when it joined the word!)

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Add '-less' to 'fear'. If you're fearless, you're incredibly brave! What's the spelling?
correct_response: FEARLESS! F-E-A-R-L-E-S-S! Without fear! That's what you are - a fearless speller!
incorrect_response: It's FEARLESS - F-E-A-R-L-E-S-S! Just add -LESS to FEAR. No tricks needed! Fear + less = fearless. Without fear!
hint: Just add -LESS straight onto the end of FEAR. No changes needed to either part!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Here's a long one! Add '-ness' to 'happy'. But watch out - something changes! What do you get?
correct_response: HAPPINESS! H-A-P-P-I-N-E-S-S! The Y changed to an I before -ness! When a word ends in Y, change it to I before adding the suffix! Happy becomes happi-ness!
incorrect_response: It's HAPPINESS - H-A-P-P-I-N-E-S-S! The Y at the end of 'happy' changes to I before we add -ness. This is a rule: when a word ends in consonant + Y, change Y to I before adding a suffix!
hint: You can't just write 'happyness'! The Y changes to... what letter? Then add -ness!
mnemonic: Y to I when adding a suffix (unless it's -ing!)
metacognitive_prompt: Why do you think the Y changes to I? English has lots of these little spelling rules!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What does the prefix 'un-' mean? If someone is UNHAPPY, what does the 'un' part tell you?
correct_response: Not! UN means NOT! Unhappy means NOT happy! Un is a tiny word part that flips the meaning! Like a switch!
incorrect_response: UN- means NOT or the OPPOSITE! So unhappy = NOT happy, unkind = NOT kind, unfair = NOT fair. It's like a magic 'not' switch at the start of a word!
hint: If you're happy and then you become UN-happy... you're not happy anymore! UN means...
mnemonic: UN = Undo! Not! The opposite!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Add 'un-' to 'fair'. What word do you get? Spell it out!
correct_response: UNFAIR! U-N-F-A-I-R! Not fair! And look - you just stick UN- on the front. The spelling of 'fair' doesn't change at all!
incorrect_response: It's UNFAIR - U-N-F-A-I-R! Just add UN- to the front of FAIR. Nothing else changes! That's the great thing about prefixes - they just attach to the front!
hint: Just put U-N right in front of FAIR. Nothing changes about the word 'fair' itself!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What's the opposite of 'lock'? If you lock a door and then you want to open it, you...
correct_response: UNLOCK! U-N-L-O-C-K! The prefix un- can also mean 'to reverse an action'! Lock... unlock! Do... undo! Tie... untie!
incorrect_response: It's UNLOCK! U-N-L-O-C-K. Add un- to the front! UN- doesn't just mean 'not' - it can also mean 'reverse the action'. Lock becomes unlock! Wrap becomes unwrap!
hint: Put the magic prefix UN- in front of lock. UN + LOCK = ...
mnemonic: UN- can mean NOT (unhappy) or REVERSE IT (unlock, undo, untie)
metacognitive_prompt: Did you notice UN- can mean two things? 'Not' (unhappy) and 'reverse it' (unlock)!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: If something is 'usual' and it happens all the time, what do we call something that doesn't happen often? Add un-!
correct_response: UNUSUAL! U-N-U-S-U-A-L! Not usual! And look - it starts with U-N-U, which looks a bit unusual itself!
incorrect_response: It's UNUSUAL - U-N-U-S-U-A-L! Not usual! Just stick un- on the front. Even though it looks funny starting with U-N-U, that's correct!
hint: Just add UN- to the front of usual. It might look funny with all those U's but that's right!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: True or false: when you add 'un-' to a word, you change the spelling of the original word. For example, does 'kind' change when it becomes 'unkind'?
correct_response: False! The word 'kind' stays exactly the same! You just put UN- in front of it: UN + KIND = UNKIND! Prefixes don't change the root word!
incorrect_response: It's false! When you add a prefix like un-, the original word stays exactly the same! UN + KIND = UNKIND. UN + HAPPY = UNHAPPY. The root word never changes!
hint: Look at 'unkind'. Can you still see the word 'kind' inside it, spelled exactly the same?
mnemonic: Prefixes just stick on the front - they don't change anything!
metacognitive_prompt: This is a really useful rule! Prefixes never change the root word's spelling!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Quick challenge! I'm going to say a word and you tell me what it means. Ready? UNPACK. What does unpack mean?
correct_response: Exactly! Unpack means to take things OUT - the opposite of packing them in! UN- reverses the action! You're a prefix pro!
incorrect_response: UNPACK means to take everything OUT of a bag or suitcase - the opposite of packing! UN- reverses what you did. Pack your bag... then unpack your bag!
hint: If you PACK a suitcase, you put things IN. So if you UN-pack...

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Homophones are words that SOUND the same but are SPELLED differently! Here's one: 'Blank going to the park.' Is it THERE, THEIR, or THEY'RE?
correct_response: THEY'RE! That's short for 'they are'. They ARE going to the park! If you can replace it with 'they are', it's always THEY'RE!
incorrect_response: It's THEY'RE! The trick is: THEY'RE is short for 'they are'. Try it: 'They are going to the park' - that works! So it's they're! Remember: THEY'RE = THEY ARE.
hint: Try replacing the blank with 'they are'. Does 'They are going to the park' make sense? If yes, use the shortened version!
mnemonic: THEY'RE = THEY ARE (look for the apostrophe - it shows letters are missing!)

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: 'The children left blank coats in the cloakroom.' Is it THERE, THEIR, or THEY'RE?
correct_response: THEIR! It means 'belonging to them'. Their coats = the coats belonging to the children! THEIR has 'heir' in it - an heir inherits things that belong to them!
incorrect_response: It's THEIR! THEIR means 'belonging to them'. Their coats, their bags, their toys. Remember: THEIR = belonging to them. And 'the' is hiding at the start: THE-IR!
hint: The coats belong to the children. Which word means 'belonging to them'? It has 'the' hiding at the start!
mnemonic: THEIR = belonging to them (THE + IR, THE is hiding inside!)

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: 'I have blank cats.' Is it TO, TOO, or TWO?
correct_response: TWO! Because it's the number 2! TWO = the number. That's the easy one to spot - if it's a number, it's always T-W-O!
incorrect_response: It's TWO! T-W-O is the NUMBER 2. TO is for direction (go TO school). TOO means 'also' or 'too much'. Two cats = 2 cats!
hint: You're counting cats here. How many? The answer is a number. Which spelling is the number?
mnemonic: TWO = the number (it has a W like tWins - there are tWo of them!)

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: 'The cake was blank sweet.' Is it TO, TOO, or TWO? The cake was really, really, REALLY sweet!
correct_response: TOO! As in 'too much'! TOO sweet, TOO hot, TOO big! TOO has an extra O because it means 'too much' - even the spelling has too many O's!
incorrect_response: It's TOO! T-O-O! When something is 'too much' or 'too many', use TOO with two O's. It's like the word itself has TOO many O's! Too sweet, too big, too loud!
hint: It means 'too much' or 'excessively'. This version has an EXTRA O - like it's got too many letters!
mnemonic: TOO has too many O's! (OO = too much!)
metacognitive_prompt: What's your trick for remembering which to/too/two to use?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: 'Put the book over blank.' Is it THERE, THEIR, or THEY'RE?
correct_response: THERE! It's about a place - over THERE! And look - HERE is hiding inside tHERE! Where? There! Here and there are both about places!
incorrect_response: It's THERE! T-H-E-R-E! This one is about a PLACE. Put it there, over there, look there! And here's a great trick: HERE is hiding inside THERE! Both are about places!
hint: This is about a PLACE - 'over ____'. Which word has HERE hiding inside it? T-HERE!
mnemonic: THERE = a place (HERE is hiding inside t-HERE!)

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: 'We went blank the shops.' Is it TO, TOO, or TWO?
correct_response: TO! Just plain T-O! We went TO the shops. TO is for direction and movement - going TO a place! The simplest one!
incorrect_response: It's TO - just T-O! TO is used for direction: go TO school, walk TO the park, went TO the shops. It's the most common one and the shortest!
hint: You're going somewhere - heading in a direction. Which is the simplest, shortest spelling?
mnemonic: TO = going TO a place (the plain, simple one for directions!)

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: 'I can blank the birds singing.' Is it HERE or HEAR?
correct_response: HEAR! H-E-A-R! You HEAR with your EAR! And look - EAR is hiding inside HEAR! That's how you remember!
incorrect_response: It's HEAR! H-E-A-R! The one about listening! Here's the trick: you HEAR with your EAR, and EAR is hiding inside HEAR! H-EAR! For listening, always use HEAR!
hint: You do this with your ears! And EAR is hiding inside one of these words... h-EAR!
mnemonic: HEAR has EAR in it - you hear with your ear!
metacognitive_prompt: Finding little words hiding inside big words is such a useful spelling trick!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: 'I got a blank bike for my birthday.' Is it KNEW or NEW?
correct_response: NEW! N-E-W! A brand new bike! NEW means not old or not used before. KNEW is the past tense of KNOW (I knew the answer)!
incorrect_response: It's NEW - N-E-W! A new bike = a brand new, shiny bike. KNEW (K-N-E-W) means you knew something in the past - 'I knew the answer.' New = not old. Knew = past of know!
hint: Is the bike old or brand new? Not old means...
mnemonic: KNEW has a silent K - it's the KN family, past tense of KNOW!
metacognitive_prompt: KNEW has the silent K pattern we learned earlier! K-N-E-W. What other KN words do you remember?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What two words make the contraction 'can't'? C-A-N-apostrophe-T. What's the long way of saying can't?
correct_response: CANNOT! Or CAN NOT! The apostrophe in can't shows where the letters N-O have been squished out! Can-not becomes can't! Clever shortcut!
incorrect_response: CAN'T is short for CANNOT! The apostrophe sits where the missing letters used to be. CAN-NOT... take out the 'no' and squish it together... CAN'T! The apostrophe marks the gap!
hint: Can't means you're NOT able to do something. It's 'can' plus 'not' squished together. Can... not... can't!
mnemonic: The apostrophe is like a tiny ghost marking where letters disappeared!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What's the short way to say 'did not'? Squish those two words together into one!
correct_response: DIDN'T! D-I-D-N-apostrophe-T! Did not becomes didn't! The 'o' disappears and the apostrophe takes its place!
incorrect_response: It's DIDN'T! D-I-D-N-apostrophe-T. Take 'did not', remove the O from 'not', and put an apostrophe where the O was. The apostrophe is the O's ghost!
hint: Take 'did not' and remove one letter from 'not'. Put an apostrophe where that letter was!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What two words make the contraction I'm? I'm happy! What's I'm the short version of?
correct_response: I AM! I'm = I am! The apostrophe replaced the 'a' in 'am'. I'm happy means I am happy!
incorrect_response: I'M is short for I AM! The A from 'am' has been removed, and the apostrophe takes its place. I + am = I'm! Try it: 'I'm hungry' means 'I am hungry'!
hint: I'm happy. I ___ happy. What word goes in the gap? I is already there, so what word is hiding?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Spell the contraction for 'do not'. D-O N-O-T squished together! What do you get?
correct_response: DON'T! D-O-N-apostrophe-T! Do not = don't! The apostrophe replaces the O in 'not'!
incorrect_response: It's DON'T! Take 'do not', squish them together, and remove the O from 'not'. Put an apostrophe where the O was. Do + n't = don't!
hint: Take 'do not', join them together, and remove the O from 'not'. Replace it with an apostrophe!
metacognitive_prompt: What's the pattern? In most contractions, the apostrophe replaces the O from 'not'!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What two words make the contraction we'll? We'll go to the beach! What's the long version?
correct_response: WE WILL! We'll = we will! The apostrophe replaces 'wi' from 'will'. We'll have a great time!
incorrect_response: WE'LL is short for WE WILL! The 'wi' from 'will' gets removed and replaced with an apostrophe. We + will = we'll! This one takes away two letters!
hint: We'll go to the beach. We _____ go to the beach. What's the future word?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Here's a tricky one! What two words make the contraction it's? And be careful - it's with an apostrophe is NOT the same as its without one!
correct_response: IT IS! Or sometimes IT HAS! It's raining means it is raining! And remember: it's with an apostrophe ALWAYS means 'it is' or 'it has'. Its without an apostrophe means 'belonging to it'!
incorrect_response: IT'S is short for IT IS (or sometimes IT HAS)! The apostrophe replaces the 'i' in 'is'. Be careful: it's with apostrophe = 'it is'. Its without apostrophe = 'belonging to it'!
hint: It's raining. It ___ raining. What little word fits? It... is!
mnemonic: IT'S = IT IS (apostrophe = something is missing!). ITS = belonging to it (no apostrophe needed!)
metacognitive_prompt: This is one of the trickiest rules in English! How will you remember the difference?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Detective time! Find the spelling mistake in this sentence: 'The cat sat on the mat becuz it was tired.' What's wrong?
correct_response: You found it! 'Becuz' should be 'because'! B-E-C-A-U-S-E! Remember: Big Elephants Can Always Understand Small Elephants! Sharp eyes!
incorrect_response: The mistake is 'becuz' - it should be BECAUSE! B-E-C-A-U-S-E. 'Becuz' is how it sounds, but the real spelling is 'because'. Big Elephants Can Always Understand Small Elephants!
hint: Look at each word carefully. One of them is spelled the way it sounds but not the way it should be written. It's a word we've practised!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What's wrong with this sentence: 'i went to the shop.' Can you spot the mistake?
correct_answers: the i needs to be capital
correct_response: The 'i' should be a CAPITAL I! When you write about yourself, 'I' is ALWAYS a capital letter! 'I went to the shop.' Eagle eyes!
incorrect_response: The 'i' at the start needs to be a CAPITAL I! 'I' should always be a capital letter whenever you write about yourself, AND the start of a sentence needs a capital letter! 'I went to the shop.'
hint: Look at the very first word. When you talk about yourself, that one-letter word should always be...
mnemonic: I is ALWAYS a capital letter - it's the most important person: you!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Find the mistake: 'My frend came to play after school.' What word is spelled wrong?
correct_response: It's 'frend' - it should be FRIEND! F-R-I-E-N-D! Remember: a friend is a friend to the END! The 'ie' is the tricky part!
incorrect_response: The mistake is 'frend' - it should be FRIEND, F-R-I-E-N-D. The I and E are in there even though you can't really hear them. A friend is a friend to the END!
hint: One word is missing some letters in the middle. It's a word about someone you play with. Check the vowels!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Spot the mistake: 'They were runing in the park.' Something's not quite right with one of those words!
correct_response: You spotted it! 'Runing' should be RUNNING with double N! R-U-N-N-I-N-G! Remember the doubling rule: short vowel + single consonant = double it before adding -ing!
incorrect_response: The mistake is 'runing' - it should be RUNNING with two N's! R-U-N-N-I-N-G. When you add -ing to 'run', you double the N because of the short vowel rule!
hint: Look at the word with -ing. When we add -ing to 'run', we need to do something to the N first. Remember the doubling rule?
metacognitive_prompt: What rule did the writer forget? Short vowel + single consonant = ...

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Find the mistake: 'There going to the cinema.' Think about those tricky homophones!
correct_response: Sharp eyes! 'There' should be 'they're'! THEY'RE going = THEY ARE going! Remember: try replacing it with 'they are'. 'They are going to the cinema' - that works!
incorrect_response: It should be THEY'RE, not THERE! 'They're going' means 'they are going'. THERE is about a place (over there). THEY'RE is short for 'they are'. Try the swap test!
hint: Is this about a place (there), belonging to them (their), or short for 'they are' (they're)? Try saying 'they are going to the cinema'...
metacognitive_prompt: The swap test really works! If you can say 'they are' instead, it must be 'they're'!

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Last detective challenge! Find TWO mistakes in this sentence: 'the dog nocked over the watir bowl.'
correct_answers: nocked should be knocked and watir should be water
correct_response: Amazing detective work! 'Nocked' should be KNOCKED with the silent K! And 'watir' should be WATER! And bonus: 'the' at the start should have a capital T! You're incredible!
incorrect_response: There are two (maybe three!) mistakes. 'Nocked' should be KNOCKED (remember the silent K in the KN family!), and 'watir' should be WATER (W-A-T-E-R). Plus 'the' at the start of a sentence needs a capital T!
hint: One word is missing a silent letter at the start (remember the KN family!), and another common word has the wrong vowel near the end!
metacognitive_prompt: When you proofread, check for silent letters, tricky vowels, and capital letters. What do you check first?
